name: Maryland
notes_normalized: Cannabis is legal in Maryland under state law for recreational adult use. Medical cannabis is also legal under a regulated state program in Maryland. Cannabis remains federally illegal in United States.
notes_raw: style="background:#196E92;" | {{Hs|1}} Cannabis in Maryland is legal for medical use and recreational use. In 2013, a state law was enacted to establish a state-regulated medical cannabis program. LaPrade Maryland Medical Cannabis Commission (MMCC), became operational on December 1, 2017. A majority of voters approved the 2022 Maryland Question 4 referendum to legalize recreational use of cannabis on November 8, 2022, with 67.2% of voters in favor and 32.8% against. Under Question 4, adults 21 years of age and older are permitted to grow up to two cannabis plants out of public view, and possess no more than 1.5 ounces of recreational cannabis on their person. Additionally, a companion bill triggered upon passage of the referendum included a provision to automatically expunge all cases in which possession of cannabis was the only charge, as well as allow for individuals incarcerated for cannabis possession to petition for resentencing. In 2010, Maryland had the fifth-highest overall arrest rate for marijuana possession in the United States, with 409 arrests per 100,000 residents, significantly higher than the national rate, which was 256 per 100,000 people. In that year, marijuana arrests made up 49.9% of all drug possession arrests in the state. Possession of between 1.5-2.5 ounces is punishable by a civil fine of up to $250, and over 2.5 ounces is punishable by up to a $1000 fine and up to 6 months in jail. In 2010, Maryland had the fifth-highest overall arrest rate for marijuana possession in the United States, with 409 arrests per 100,000 residents, significantly higher than the national rate, which was 256 per 100,000 people. In April 2014, Governor Martin O'Malley signed a law that decriminalized the possession of 10 grams or less of marijuana. Previously, possessing such was a misdemeanor with a $500 fine and/or 90 days in jail. Under the law, people over the age of 21 who are accused of having less than 10 grams had to pay a fine not exceeding $100 for first-time offenders, $250 for second-time offenders, and $500 for third or subsequent offenders. In 2016, the Maryland General Assembly, controlled by Democrats, passed SB 517, which decriminalized the possession of marijuana paraphernalia (such as rolling papers, pipes and bongs) and decriminalized the smoking of marijuana in public. The measure makes both civil offenses punishable by a fine of up to $500. State (2019), the Maryland Court of Appeals determined that "the mere odor of marijuana coupled with possession of what is clearly less than ten grams of marijuana, absent other circumstances," is not sufficient probable cause for police to arrest and search a person within the state. From January 1, 2023, until June 30, 2023, decriminalization was temporarily expanded from possession of less than 1 ounce (28 grams) to less than 1.5 ounces (42 grams), as a civil infraction subject to a $100 fine as provided by House Bill 837, prior to full legalized recreational use of 1.5 ounces or less on July 1, 2023, due to the passage of the 2022 Maryland Question 4 referendum. On May 22, 2003, Governor Bob Ehrlich signed a bill into a law that took effect on October 1, 2003, that instituted a maximum fine of $100 for people using marijuana for pain relief.

state_modifiers.federal_conflict: Cannabis remains federally illegal in United States.
